title: policy::wild_match_opt is dead-easy to misread — semantic asymmetry is undocumented at the call site
description:
## Severity
Trivial
## Dimension
kiss
## Location
src/plugins/policy.rs:164-170, src/plugins/policy.rs:121-160
## Violation
The helper is named "wild_match_opt" but the rule encoded is "rule=None wildcards everything, rule=Some requires candidate=Some-and-equal". The asymmetry is critical (see the `ref_must_match_when_rule_specifies_it` test which exists exactly because the misreading is plausible) but the call sites use it bare. Quoted lines:

    fn wild_match_opt(candidate: Option<&String>, rule: Option<&String>) -> bool {
        match (candidate, rule) {
            (_, None) => true,
            (Some(c), Some(r)) => c == r,
            (None, Some(_)) => false,
        }
    }
    ...
    r1.eq_ignore_ascii_case(r2) && wild_match_opt(ref1.as_ref(), ref2.as_ref()) && wild_match_opt(p1.as_ref(), p2.as_ref()),

A reader skimming the call site cannot tell whether the `git_ref` field on the policy entry or on the candidate is the "rule" without going to the helper. Argument-order bugs at call sites — swapping candidate and rule — would invert the semantics silently and a test would only catch the most obvious case.
## Mandated refactor
Rename to `rule_matches_candidate(rule: Option<&String>, candidate: Option<&String>) -> bool` and put `rule` first; or introduce two newtypes (`Rule<&String>`, `Candidate<&String>`) so the type system catches the swap. Add a `///` doc comment showing the truth table.
## Filed from QA review dispatch
